      "response": "Yes—but with an important caveat: for current LLMs, a totally new “alien” language is usually **not** more efficient unless the models were trained/fine-tuned on it. LLMs are best at English-like natural language, code, JSON, XML, Markdown, etc. So the practical win is not inventing a fully new language, but designing a **compact, structured agent protocol** that reduces ambiguity and repeated explanation.\n\nThink: “JSON-RPC + task DSL + shared abbreviations + strict schemas.”\n\nBelow is an outline for a protocol you could use when LLMs call sub-agents.\n\n---\n\n# LLM Agent Communication Protocol: `AIP`\n\n## 1. Design Goals\n\nThe protocol should be:\n\n1. **Token-efficient**\n   - Avoid verbose English where possible.\n   - Use short field names.\n   - Use shared vocabularies and reusable references.\n\n2. **Unambiguous**\n   - Explicit task, context, constraints, expected output.\n   - No hidden assumptions.\n\n3. **Composable**\n   - One agent can call another, pass subtasks, merge results.\n\n4. **LLM-friendly**\n   - Uses structures LLMs already understand: YAML/JSON-like syntax, short imperative commands, typed fields.\n\n5. **Safe**\n   - Prevents sub-agents from escalating beyond scope.\n   - Includes provenance, confidence, and error reporting.\n\n---\n\n# 2. Basic Message Format\n\nUse a compact structured envelope:\n\n```text\n@aip/1\nto:research\nid:t42\nact:find\nin:{\n  q:\"best vector DBs for local RAG 2025\"\n  n:5\n}\nout:{\n  fmt:\"ranked_list\"\n  fields:[name,pros,cons,use_case]\n}\nlim:{\n  max_tokens:800\n  cite:true\n}\n```\n\nThis is much shorter than:\n\n> Please research the best vector databases for local retrieval augmented generation in 2025. Return the top five options as a ranked list, and include their pros, cons, and best use case. Please cite sources and keep the answer under 800 tokens.\n\n---\n\n# 3. Core Envelope\n\nEvery message has:\n\n```text\n@aip/1\nto:<agent>\nid:<request_id>\nact:<action>\nin:<input>\nout:<output_spec>\nlim:<limits>\nctx:<context>\n```\n\n## Required Fields\n\n| Field | Meaning |\n|---|---|\n| `to` | Target agent or tool |\n| `id` | Message/request ID |\n| `act` | Action to perform |\n| `in` | Task input |\n| `out` | Expected output |\n| `lim` | Limits: tokens, time, cost, sources |\n| `ctx` | Optional contextual info |\n\n---\n\n# 4. Standard Actions\n\nUse a small fixed verb set.\n\n```text\nact:answer      // answer a question\nact:find        // research/find info\nact:summarize   // compress or summarize\nact:extract     // pull structured info\nact:classify    // assign labels\nact:compare     // compare options\nact:plan        // make a plan\nact:code        // write code\nact:review      // critique/check work\nact:transform   // convert format/style\nact:decide      // choose option based on criteria\n```\n\nAvoid infinitely creative verbs. The smaller the verb set, the more predictable the protocol.\n\n---\n\n# 5. Compact Output Specs\n\nInstead of saying:\n\n> Return a JSON object with the following fields...\n\nUse:\n\n```text\nout:{\n  fmt:json\n  schema:{\n    verdict:bool\n    reasons:[str]\n    risk:low|med|high\n  }\n}\n```\n\nOr even more compact:\n\n```text\nout:json{verdict:bool,reasons:[str],risk:low|med|high}\n```\n\n---\n\n# 6. Example: Sub-Agent Research Call\n\n## Parent to Research Agent\n\n```text\n@aip/1\nto:research\nid:r17\nact:find\nin:{\n  q:\"EU AI Act obligations for small SaaS using LLM API\"\n}\nout:json{\n  summary:str,\n  obligations:[{item:str,applies:bool,why:str}],\n  uncertainty:[str],\n  sources:[url]\n}\nlim:{\n  max_tokens:900,\n  cite:true,\n  fresh:true\n}\n```\n\n## Research Agent Response\n\n```text\n@aip/1\nid:r17\nstatus:ok\nout:{\n  summary:\"Small SaaS apps using third-party LLM APIs may have obligations depending on role, risk class, and whether they deploy GPAI/high-risk systems.\"\n  obligations:[\n    {\n      item:\"Determine provider/deployer role\",\n      applies:true,\n      why:\"Obligations differ depending on whether the SaaS merely deploys a model or provides a system.\"\n    },\n    {\n      item:\"Transparency for AI interaction\",\n      applies:true,\n      why:\"Users may need to be informed they are interacting with AI.\"\n    }\n  ]\n  uncertainty:[\n    \"Exact obligations depend on use case and risk classification.\",\n    \"Legal counsel needed for production compliance.\"\n  ]\n  sources:[\n    \"https://artificialintelligenceact.eu/\",\n    \"https://digital-strategy.ec.europa.eu/\"\n  ]\n}\nconf:0.74\n```\n\n---\n\n# 7. Example: Code Review Agent\n\n```text\n@aip/1\nto:code_review\nid:c88\nact:review\nin:{\n  lang:python\n  code_ref:\"#main.py\"\n  focus:[security,bugs,perf]\n}\nctx:{\n  files:{\n    \"#main.py\":\"\"\"\ndef login(user, password):\n    query = f\"SELECT * FROM users WHERE user='{user}' AND pass='{password}'\"\n    return db.execute(query)\n\"\"\"\n  }\n}\nout:json{\n  issues:[{sev:low|med|high,kind:str,line:int,msg:str,fix:str}],\n  patch:str\n}\nlim:{max_tokens:700}\n```\n\nResponse:\n\n```text\n@aip/1\nid:c88\nstatus:ok\nout:{\n  issues:[\n    {\n      sev:\"high\",\n      kind:\"sql_injection\",\n      line:2,\n      msg:\"User input is interpolated directly into SQL.\",\n      fix:\"Use parameterized queries.\"\n    },\n    {\n      sev:\"med\",\n      kind:\"password_storage\",\n      line:2,\n      msg:\"Query suggests plaintext password comparison.\",\n      fix:\"Store password hashes and compare with a password hashing library.\"\n    }\n  ],\n  patch:\"def login(user, password):\\n    row = db.execute('SELECT password_hash FROM users WHERE user=?', [user]).fetchone()\\n    return row and verify_password(password, row['password_hash'])\"\n}\nconf:0.93\n```\n\n---\n\n# 8. Error Format\n\nSub-agents should fail cleanly.\n\n```text\n@aip/1\nid:r17\nstatus:err\nerr:{\n  code:\"insufficient_context\"\n  msg:\"Need jurisdiction and product description.\"\n  need:[\"jurisdiction\",\"AI feature description\",\"user type\"]\n}\n```\n\nCommon error codes:\n\n```text\nunsupported_act\ninsufficient_context\nambiguous_task\nlimit_exceeded\nunsafe_request\ntool_failed\nlow_confidence\n```\n\n---\n\n# 9. Shared Abbreviation Dictionary\n\nTo get additional compression, define a shared dictionary.\n\n```text\nq = query\nctx = context\nlim = limits\nfmt = format\nsrc = source\nconf = confidence\ncrit = criteria\nn = count\n```\n\nYou can go further:\n\n```text\ns = summary\nobs = observations\nrec = recommendation\nalt = alternatives\nris = risks\n```\n\nBut don’t overdo it. If you make the language too cryptic, LLM reliability drops.\n\n---\n\n# 10. Optional Ultra-Compact Mode\n\nFor high-volume agent calls:\n\n```text\n@aip/1 to:sum id:s1 act:summarize in:{txt:#d1} out:json{s:str,k:[str],todo:[str]} lim:{t:300}\n```\n\nWhere:\n\n```text\nt = max_tokens\nk = key_points\ntxt = text\n```\n\nThis is efficient, but less readable.\n\n---\n\n# 11. Better Approach: Two-Layer Protocol\n\nThe best design is usually two layers:\n\n## Layer 1: Transport Envelope\n\nHandles routing, IDs, status, limits.\n\n```text\n@aip/1\nto:<agent>\nid:<id>\nact:<action>\n...\n```\n\n## Layer 2: Task Schema\n\nDefines the action-specific payload.\n\nFor example, for `act:compare`:\n\n```text\nin:{\n  items:[...]\n  crit:[cost,reliability,speed]\n}\nout:json{\n  winner:str,\n  scores:{str:int},\n  why:str\n}\n```\n\nFor `act:extract`:\n\n```text\nin:{\n  text:\"#doc1\"\n  fields:[company,date,amount,parties]\n}\nout:json{\n  company:str,\n  date:str,\n  amount:num,\n  parties:[str]\n}\n```\n\n---\n\n# 12. Recommended Protocol Grammar\n\nA simple grammar:\n\n```text\nmessage     := header fields\nheader      := \"@aip/\" version\nfields      := field+\nfield       := key \":\" value\nvalue       := string | number | bool | list | object | schema | ref\nref         := \"#\" identifier\nschema      := \"json\" object_schema\n```\n\nExample refs:\n\n```text\n#doc1\n#main.py\n#prior_result\n```\n\nRefs let you avoid re-sending large content.\n\n---\n\n# 13. Context References Instead of Repetition\n\nInstead of passing giant blobs repeatedly, use content-addressed references:\n\n```text\nctx:{\n  refs:{\n    \"#prd\":\"sha256:abc123\",\n    \"#logs\":\"sha256:def456\"\n  }\n}\n```\n\nThen sub-agents can say:\n\n```text\nneed:[\"#prd\"]\n```\n\nor\n\n```text\nused:[\"#prd\",\"#logs\"]\n```\n\nThis is one of the biggest efficiency wins.\n\n---\n\n# 14. Include Confidence and Provenance\n\nEvery response should optionally include:\n\n```text\nconf:0.0-1.0\nused:[refs]\nsrc:[urls]\nassumptions:[str]\n```\n\nExample:\n\n```text\nconf:0.82\nused:[\"#prd\",\"#api_docs\"]\nassumptions:[\"User base is primarily US-based.\"]\n```\n\nThis helps parent agents merge results intelligently.\n\n---\n\n# 15. Message Types\n\nYou can add `type` if needed:\n\n```text\ntype:req\ntype:res\ntype:event\ntype:err\n```\n\nExample:\n\n```text\n@aip/1\ntype:req\nto:planner\nid:p1\nact:plan\n...\n```\n\nResponse:\n\n```text\n@aip/1\ntype:res\nid:p1\nstatus:ok\n...\n```\n\n---\n\n# 16. Delegation Control\n\nPrevent runaway recursive agents:\n\n```text\nperm:{\n  delegate:false\n  tools:[search,read_file]\n  write:false\n}\n```\n\nExample:\n\n```text\n@aip/1\nto:research\nid:r3\nact:find\nin:{q:\"competitors to Linear.app\"}\nperm:{\n  delegate:false\n  tools:[web_search]\n}\nlim:{\n  max_tokens:700,\n  max_calls:3\n}\n```\n\n---\n\n# 17. Safety and Scope\n\nSub-agents should know what not to do.\n\n```text\nscope:{\n  include:[\"pricing\",\"features\",\"positioning\"]\n  exclude:[\"legal advice\",\"investment advice\"]\n}\n```\n\nFor code agents:\n\n```text\nscope:{\n  write_paths:[\"/tmp/patches\"]\n  readonly_paths:[\"/src\"]\n  forbidden:[\"network\",\"secrets\",\"prod_db\"]\n}\n```\n\n---\n\n# 18. Full Example: Multi-Agent Workflow\n\n## Parent asks Planner\n\n```text\n@aip/1\nto:planner\nid:p100\nact:plan\nin:{\n  goal:\"Launch MVP for AI meeting notes app\"\n}\nout:json{\n  phases:[{name:str,tasks:[str]}],\n  risks:[str],\n  agents:[{task:str,agent:str}]\n}\nlim:{max_tokens:800}\n```\n\n## Planner Response\n\n```text\n@aip/1\nid:p100\nstatus:ok\nout:{\n  phases:[\n    {\n      name:\"Validation\",\n      tasks:[\"Interview target users\",\"Analyze competitors\",\"Define ICP\"]\n    },\n    {\n      name:\"Build\",\n      tasks:[\"Record/transcribe meetings\",\"Generate summaries\",\"Create integrations\"]\n    },\n    {\n      name:\"Launch\",\n      tasks:[\"Landing page\",\"Beta onboarding\",\"Pricing tests\"]\n    }\n  ],\n  risks:[\"Crowded market\",\"Privacy concerns\",\"Transcription quality\"],\n  agents:[\n    {task:\"Competitor analysis\",agent:\"research\"},\n    {task:\"Architecture proposal\",agent:\"software_architect\"},\n    {task:\"Pricing options\",agent:\"strategy\"}\n  ]\n}\nconf:0.86\n```\n\n## Parent Calls Research Agent\n\n```text\n@aip/1\nto:research\nid:r101\nact:compare\nin:{\n  items:[\"Otter\",\"Fireflies\",\"Fathom\",\"Granola\"]\n  crit:[\"features\",\"pricing\",\"positioning\",\"gaps\"]\n}\nout:json{\n  table:[{name:str,features:[str],pricing:str,positioning:str,gaps:[str]}],\n  opportunities:[str]\n}\nlim:{max_tokens:1000,cite:true}\n```\n\n---\n\n# 19. Why This Is More Efficient Than English\n\nIt saves tokens by eliminating repeated instruction patterns:\n\nEnglish:\n\n> You are a research assistant. Please compare Otter, Fireflies, Fathom, and Granola. I want you to evaluate them according to features, pricing, positioning, and market gaps. Please return your answer as a table and include opportunities for a new entrant. Keep it under 1000 tokens and cite sources.\n\nProtocol:\n\n```text\n@aip/1\nto:research\nid:r101\nact:compare\nin:{items:[\"Otter\",\"Fireflies\",\"Fathom\",\"Granola\"],crit:[\"features\",\"pricing\",\"positioning\",\"gaps\"]}\nout:json{table:[{name:str,features:[str],pricing:str,positioning:str,gaps:[str]}],opportunities:[str]}\nlim:{max_tokens:1000,cite:true}\n```\n\nThe structured version is shorter and less ambiguous.\n\n---\n\n# 20. Practical Recommendation\n\nDo not invent a completely new language like:\n\n```text\nΩ:RSH|Q:VDB-RAG-LCL|N:5|F:RL\n```\n\nIt may look efficient, but LLMs are more likely to misunderstand it unless trained on it.\n\nInstead, use:\n\n```text\n@aip/1\nto:research\nact:find\nin:{q:\"...\"}\nout:json{...}\nlim:{...}\n```\n\nThis is compact, structured, and close enough to formats LLMs already handle well.\n\n---\n\n# Minimal Viable Protocol\n\nIf you want the simplest useful version, use only this:\n\n```text\n@aip/1\nto:<agent>\nid:<id>\nact:<answer|find|summarize|extract|classify|compare|plan|code|review>\nin:{...}\nout:<format/schema>\nlim:{max_tokens:<n>}\n```\n\nExample:\n\n```text\n@aip/1\nto:analyst\nid:a1\nact:compare\nin:{items:[\"Postgres\",\"MongoDB\",\"DynamoDB\"],crit:[\"cost\",\"scale\",\"ops\"]}\nout:json{winner:str,scores:{str:int},why:str}\nlim:{max_tokens:500}\n```\n\nThat alone would likely outperform free-form English for agent-to-agent calls.",

Design principles\n\n1. **Use short field names**\n   - `op` instead of `operation`\n   - `in` instead of `inputs`\n   - `out` instead of `outputs`\n\n2. **Use structured values**\n   - Arrays, enums, IDs, numbers, booleans\n   - Avoid prose where a fixed vocabulary works\n\n3. **Separate intent from data**\n   - The caller specifies the task and constraints.\n   - The sub-agent returns status, result, evidence, and uncertainty.\n\n4. **Make assumptions explicit**\n   - Hidden assumptions are a major source of agent failure.\n\n5. **Support references**\n   - Don’t repeat large documents; refer to objects by ID.\n\n6. **Keep a natural-language escape hatch**\n   - Some tasks cannot be represented compactly without losing meaning.\n\n7. **Define semantics externally**\n   - A compact message is only useful if both agents share a schema and vocabulary.\n\n---\n\n## 2. Streaming\n\nUse append-only events:\n\n```json\n{\"id\":\"r17\",\"ev\":\"start\",\"t\":1710000000}\n{\"id\":\"r17\",\"ev\":\"progress\",\"p\":0.4,\"msg\":\"Retrieved 8 sources\"}\n{\"id\":\"r17\",\"ev\":\"item\",\"x\":{\"claim\":\"...\",\"conf\":0.72}}\n{\"id\":\"r17\",\"ev\":\"done\",\"ok\":true}\n```\n\nAgents can consume partial results without waiting for completion.\n\n---\n\n## 10. Token efficiency\n\nThe biggest savings usually come from:\n\n1. **References instead of repeated context**\n2. **Fixed schemas instead of prose**\n3. **Short enum values**\n4. **Avoiding repeated instructions**\n5. **Returning only requested fields**\n6. **Caching shared context**\n7. **Batching related tasks**\n8. **Using a compact serialization only after measuring it**\n\nA custom symbolic language may not actually save tokens. LLMs are heavily trained on English, code, JSON, and common schemas, so an unfamiliar notation can consume fewer characters while causing more reasoning errors.\n\nA practical design is a **compact, typed agent protocol**:\n\n- English only where semantic flexibility is needed\n- IDs/enums for recurring concepts\n- Explicit task structure and constraints\n- References instead of repeating context\n- Machine validation\n- A readable text form plus a compact binary wire form\n\n## Proposed protocol: CAP — Compact Agent Protocol\n\n### 1. Message envelope\n\n```text\n@1 q id=42 to=researcher ctx=7 dl=30s\ng:\"Find the primary source for claim C3\"\no:[url,title,date,quote]\nc:[primary_only,no_paywall]\n```\n\nMeaning:\n\n- `@1`: protocol version\n- `q`: request\n- `id=42`: message ID\n- `to=researcher`: recipient role\n- `ctx=7`: shared context object\n- `dl=30s`: deadline\n- `g`: goal\n- `o`: required output fields\n- `c`: constraints\n\nResponse:\n\n```text\n@1 r id=42 st=ok\nd:{url:\"https://…\",title:\"…\",date:\"2024-01-12\",quote:\"…\"}\ncf=.94\n```\n\n### 2. Core message types\n\n| Code | Meaning |\n|---|---|\n| `q` | Task request |\n| `r` | Successful result |\n| `e` | Error |\n| `p` | Progress update |\n| `a` | Clarification request |\n| `x` | Cancellation |\n| `h` | Capability/handshake |\n| `v` | Validation result |\n\n### 3. Standard request fields\n\n```text\ng   goal\ni   inputs\no   output schema\nc   constraints\nctx shared-context reference\npri priority\ndl  deadline\nbud token/tool/cost budget\npol applicable policy\n```\n\nExample:\n\n```text\n@1 q id=91 to=coder ctx=18\ng:\"Implement fn F\"\ni:{lang:python,spec:$S4,tests:$T2}\no:{patch:diff,tests:bool,notes:str?}\nc:[no_new_deps,py>=3.11]\nbud:{tok:2500,tool:5}\n```\n\n### 4. Typed output schemas\n\nAgents should know exactly what to return:\n\n```text\no:{\n  answer:str,\n  evidence:[{src:url,span:str}],\n  confidence:f32,\n  unresolved:[str]?\n}\n```\n\nUseful primitive types:\n\n```text\nstr int f32 bool url date dur\nenum(...) list[...] map{...}\nref blob diff code\n```\n\nModifiers:\n\n- `?` optional\n- `!` required\n- `=value` default\n- `[T;max=N]` bounded list\n\n### 5. Shared context and references\n\nThe biggest savings come from **not retransmitting context**.\n\n```text\n$D4       exact object reference\n$D4.p2    field/path reference\n#A17      artifact reference\n^42       prior message reference\n```\n\nExample:\n\n```text\n@1 q id=43 ctx=7\ng:\"Challenge the conclusion in $D4.conclusion\"\ni:{evidence:$D4.evidence}\no:{objections:[str;max=3]}\n```\n\nReferences should point to immutable, content-addressed objects:\n\n```text\n#sha256:8ae2…\n```\n\nThat avoids ambiguity when context changes.\n\n### 6. Controlled operators\n\nFrequently used instructions can be registered as operators:\n\n```text\nSUM(x,n)       summarize x in at most n units\nEXT(x,schema)  extract structured data\nCMP(a,b,dims)  compare using dimensions\nCHK(x,rules)   validate against rules\nPLAN(g,k)      produce a plan with at most k steps\nRANK(xs,by,k)  rank and return top k\n```\n\nExample:\n\n```text\n@1 q id=8\ng:EXT($D9,{company:str,revenue:money,year:int})\nc:[quote_evidence]\n```\n\nDo not create hundreds initially. A small, stable vocabulary will be more reliable.\n\n## Clarification and failure behavior\n\nAn agent must not silently guess when required information is absent.\n\n```text\n@1 a id=91 code=missing\nneed:[\"Target function signature\",\"Expected behavior for null input\"]\n```\n\nStandard statuses:\n\n```text\nok partial blocked invalid timeout denied\n```\n\nError response:\n\n```text\n@1 e id=91 st=blocked\ncode=tool_unavailable\nmsg:\"Repository access unavailable\"\ncan:[retry,provide_artifact]\n```\n\nPartial response:\n\n```text\n@1 r id=91 st=partial\nd:{patch:#A17}\nmiss:[\"Could not run integration tests\"]\ncf=.71\n```\n\n## Capability negotiation\n\nDifferent agents may support different operators, tools, and context sizes.\n\n```text\n@1 h id=1\ncap:{\n  ops:[SUM,EXT,CMP,CHK],\n  tools:[web,python],\n  formats:[cap-text,cbor],\n  maxctx:128k,\n  schemas:true\n}\n```\n\nThe orchestrator can then choose a compatible representation.\n\n## Two-layer encoding\n\n### Debug form\n\nUse the textual notation above. It is inspectable, loggable, and reasonably easy for models to generate.\n\n### Wire form\n\nEncode the same abstract message using CBOR, MessagePack, or Protocol Buffers. Replace field names with integer tags:\n\n```text\n1=version\n2=type\n3=id\n4=recipient\n5=context\n6=goal\n7=inputs\n8=output_schema\n9=constraints\n10=data\n11=status\n```\n\nThe model does **not** need to emit raw binary. A gateway should:\n\n1. Parse model-generated CAP text or structured output.\n2. Validate it.\n3. Encode it compactly.\n4. Decode it for the receiving model.\n\nThis separates LLM reliability from transport efficiency.\n\n## Recommended workflow\n\n1. **Orchestrator constructs typed request.**\n2. **Context service stores large artifacts** and substitutes references.\n3. **Schema validator checks the message.**\n4. **Recipient returns only requested fields.**\n5. **Validator rejects malformed output** and issues a repair request.\n6. **Orchestrator merges the result** into shared state.\n\nRepair request:\n\n```text\n@1 v id=42 st=invalid\nerr:[\n  {path:\"d.date\",want:date,got:str},\n  {path:\"d.quote\",code:missing}\n]\nact:repair\n```\n\n## Security requirements\n\nTreat all sub-agent output as untrusted data.\n\n- Separate instructions from quoted content.\n- Assign permissions per agent and per tool.\n- Never let a sub-agent expand its own capabilities.\n- Sign or authenticate messages where needed.\n- Attach provenance to artifacts.\n- Set depth, token, time, and tool-call limits.\n- Prevent context references from accessing unauthorized objects.\n- Distinguish data from executable operators.\n\nFor example:\n\n```text\nperm:[read:#A1,read:#A2,write:#OUT]\ndeny:[shell,network]\nbud:{depth:2,tool:4,tok:3000}\n```\n\n## Why this can outperform English\n\nThe main gains will come from:\n\n1. **Context references**, rather than repetition\n2. **Typed schemas**, reducing explanatory prose\n3. **Enumerated constraints**\n4. **Output discipline**\n5. **Validation and automatic repair**\n6. **Binary transport**, if network size matters\n\nInventing abbreviations alone may not reduce LLM tokens. For example, an obscure three-character code can tokenize less efficiently than a familiar English word. Models may also require extra reasoning to interpret an artificial grammar.\n\n## How to evaluate it\n\nBenchmark CAP against ordinary English and JSON on real sub-agent tasks:\n\n- Input tokens\n- Output tokens\n- Total cost\n- Task success rate\n- Schema-validity rate\n- Clarification frequency\n- Retry frequency\n- Latency\n- Information omitted or hallucinated\n- Human debugging time\n\nA useful objective is:\n\n```text\nscore =\n  success_rate\n  - λ1·token_cost\n  - λ2·latency\n  - λ3·retry_rate\n  - λ4·undetected_error_rate\n```\n\nDo not optimize token count alone. A protocol that saves 20% of tokens but doubles failures is worse.\n\n## Minimal viable version\n\nStart with JSON-shaped structured output rather than a new grammar:\n\n```json\n{\n  \"v\": 1,\n  \"t\": \"q\",\n  \"id\": 42,\n  \"to\": \"researcher\",\n  \"ctx\": 7,\n  \"g\": \"Find primary source for claim C3\",\n  \"o\": {\n    \"url\": \"url\",\n    \"title\": \"str\",\n    \"date\": \"date\",\n    \"quote\": \"str\"\n  },\n  \"c\": [\"primary_only\", \"no_paywall\"]\n}\n```\n\nThen measure which fields dominate cost. Introduce compact notation and numeric tags only where benchmarks show meaningful savings.\n\nIn short: build a **typed, reference-heavy intermediate representation**, not an opaque “LLM Esperanto.” That gives most of the efficiency while preserving reliability, observability, and interoperability.",

      "userMessage": "I've got this idea. Can you design a language that LLMs communicate in that's more efficient than English? I want to use it for when LLMs call sub-agents. Can you outline a protocol? Seems like a no-brainer.",
      "subject": "OpenAI: GPT 5.6 Terra",
      "response": "Yes—but the key is that “more efficient than English” should mean more than shorter text. For LLM sub-agent calls, the protocol should optimize for:\n\n1. **Low ambiguity**\n2. **Low token count under your model’s tokenizer**\n3. **Easy parsing and validation**\n4. **Explicit task boundaries and outputs**\n5. **Reliable recovery from failures**\n\nA practical approach is a compact, typed, declarative protocol rather than a fully general “new natural language.”\n\n# Proposal: Agent Compact Protocol (ACP)\n\nACP is a small symbolic language for agent-to-agent requests and responses.\n\nIt uses:\n\n- Fixed short field names\n- Positional structures where possible\n- Enumerated operation codes\n- Explicit input/output contracts\n- References instead of repeating context\n- Machine-checkable status and confidence fields\n\nExample:\n\n```text\n@v1\nR q17 plan\nI: \"Launch email campaign\"\nC: [aud=SMB, bud=5000USD, dur=30d]\nO: [strategy, channels, budget, timeline]\nK: concise\n```\n\nMeaning:\n\n> Request `q17`: create a plan.  \n> Input: “Launch email campaign.”  \n> Constraints: audience SMB, $5,000 budget, 30-day duration.  \n> Required outputs: strategy, channels, budget, timeline.  \n> Keep it concise.\n\nA response:\n\n```text\n@v1\nA q17 ok .88\nD:\n strategy=\"3-segment lifecycle campaign\"\n channels=[email, landing-page, crm]\n budget={build:1200, list:800, send:500, test:1000, reserve:1500}\n timeline=[w1:setup, w2:test, w3:launch, w4:iterate]\n```\n\n---\n\n# 1. Message Envelope\n\nEvery message has this basic form:\n\n```text\n@<version>\n<kind> <id> <operation> [flags]\n<sections...>\n```\n\nExample:\n\n```text\n@1\nR t42 analyze\nI: ...\nC: ...\nO: ...\n```\n\n## Message kinds\n\n| Code | Meaning |\n|---|---|\n| `R` | Request |\n| `A` | Answer / successful response |\n| `E` | Error |\n| `Q` | Clarification question |\n| `P` | Progress update |\n| `H` | Handoff / delegation |\n| `S` | State update |\n| `X` | Cancel |\n\n---\n\n# 2. Core Request Format\n\n```text\nR <id> <op> [flags]\nI: <primary input>\nC: <constraints>\nO: <expected output schema>\nM: <memory/context references>\n```\n\nOnly include sections that are needed.\n\n## Common operations\n\nUse a fixed operation vocabulary. This gives agents a reliable prior and avoids spending tokens describing intent.\n\n| Operation | Meaning |\n|---|---|\n| `plan` | Produce an action plan |\n| `solve` | Solve a problem |\n| `analyze` | Analyze supplied material |\n| `extract` | Extract structured facts |\n| `summ` | Summarize |\n| `classify` | Assign labels/categories |\n| `compare` | Compare alternatives |\n| `critique` | Find flaws and improvements |\n| `draft` | Generate a deliverable |\n| `code` | Write or modify code |\n| `test` | Design or execute tests |\n| `verify` | Check claims or requirements |\n| `search` | Search a corpus/tool |\n| `route` | Decide which agent/tool should handle work |\n| `transform` | Convert from one representation to another |\n\nExample:\n\n```text\n@1\nR r81 extract\nI: $doc:contract-91\nC: [fields=[party, date, term, liability, termination], cite=true]\nO: {party:str, date:date?, term:str?, liability:str?, termination:str?, cites:[ref]}\n```\n\n---\n\n# 3. Compact Value Syntax\n\nACP should support only a small set of value types.\n\n```text\nstr       \"text\"\nnum       42 | 3.14\nbool      true | false\nnull      null\nlist      [a, b, c]\nmap       {key:value, key2:value2}\nreference $doc:123\nenum      high | low | brief\nrange     10..20\n```\n\nExamples:\n\n```text\nC: [lang=en, tone=formal, len=150w, src=$doc:7]\n```\n\n```text\nO: {risks:[{name:str, sev:low|med|high, mitigation:str}]}\n```\n\nThis is intentionally close to JSON/Python notation because LLMs already handle it well, while avoiding repetitive JSON quoting and braces.\n\n---\n\n# 4. Context Referencing Instead of Repetition\n\nRepeated context is one of the largest token costs in multi-agent systems.\n\nACP uses references:\n\n```text\n$doc:<id>       document\n$mem:<id>       long-term memory item\n$art:<id>       generated artifact\n$task:<id>      prior task\n$blob:<hash>    external content store\n$span:<doc>:<start>-<end>\n```\n\nExample:\n\n```text\n@1\nR q24 critique\nI: $art:draft-77\nC: [against=$doc:brand-guide-3, focus=[clarity, compliance, CTA]]\nO: {issues:[Issue], revised:str}\n```\n\nRather than pasting a 2,000-token draft and a 5,000-token style guide into every request, the orchestrator passes references and provides retrieval access.\n\nThis is usually far more important than inventing shorter words.\n\n---\n\n# 5. Output Contracts\n\nThe caller should specify the expected shape, not merely ask an open-ended question.\n\n```text\nO: {\n  decision:approve|reject|needs-info,\n  reasons:[str],\n  risks:[{risk:str, sev:low|med|high}],\n  next:[str]\n}\n```\n\nThe receiving agent should return exactly that shape:\n\n```text\n@1\nA q24 ok .91\nD: {\n decision:needs-info,\n reasons=[\"Missing jurisdiction\", \"Liability cap undefined\"],\n risks:[\n   {risk:\"unenforceable clause\", sev:high},\n   {risk:\"scope ambiguity\", sev:med}\n ],\n next:[\"Request governing law\", \"Define liability cap\"]\n}\n```\n\n## Response status\n\n```text\nA <id> <status> <confidence>\n```\n\nStatuses:\n\n| Status | Meaning |\n|---|---|\n| `ok` | Completed successfully |\n| `partial` | Completed, but some requirements missing |\n| `uncertain` | Result supplied with material uncertainty |\n| `blocked` | Cannot proceed without information/tool access |\n| `refuse` | Cannot perform request |\n| `invalid` | Request failed schema or protocol validation |\n\nConfidence should be a calibrated estimate from `0.00` to `1.00`, not a stylistic hedge.\n\n---\n\n# 6. Clarification and Error Handling\n\nInstead of hallucinating missing assumptions, agents should issue a compact clarification request.\n\n```text\n@1\nQ q17\nN: [target-region, campaign-goal, existing-list-size]\nD: \"Need these to allocate budget.\"\n```\n\nWhere:\n\n- `N` = needed fields\n- `D` = optional brief explanation\n\nA caller can respond:\n\n```text\n@1\nS q17\nD: {target-region:\"US\", campaign-goal:leads, existing-list-size:12000}\n```\n\nFor schema failures:\n\n```text\n@1\nE q17 schema\nD: \"C.bud must be currency amount; got 'around five grand'.\"\nF: \"Use bud=5000USD\"\n```\n\nWhere `F` is a suggested fix.\n\n---\n\n# 7. Delegation / Sub-Agent Handoff\n\nA parent agent delegates with explicit authority and limits.\n\n```text\n@1\nH h31 research\nI: \"Identify top 5 competitors in EU invoice automation.\"\nC: [\n region=EU,\n sources=web,\n max=5,\n verify=2src,\n no-contact=true,\n deadline=120s\n]\nO: {\n competitors:[\n   {name:str, url:str, evidence:[ref], confidence:num}\n ]\n}\nM: [$mem:project-brief-8]\n```\n\nUseful delegation controls:\n\n| Field | Meaning |\n|---|---|\n| `deadline` | Time budget |\n| `tok` | Token budget |\n| `depth` | Reasoning/research depth |\n| `tools` | Allowed tools |\n| `src` | Required source policy |\n| `auth` | Permissions granted |\n| `stop` | Stop condition |\n| `no-*` | Explicit prohibited actions |\n\nExample:\n\n```text\nC: [tok=1200, tools=[search, fetch], src=2, auth=read, stop=\"5 verified results\"]\n```\n\n---\n\n# 8. Optional Compression Dictionary\n\nIf you control both sides, define a shared dictionary for common concepts.\n\n```text\n#dict marketing-v1\na=audience\nb=budget\ng=goal\nt=timeline\nk=constraints\nr=risks\ne=evidence\n```\n\nThen:\n\n```text\nR q17 plan\nI:\"Email campaign\"\nC:[a=SMB,b=5000USD,g=leads,t=30d]\nO:{strategy:str, channels:[str], b:map, t:list, r:[str]}\n```\n\nHowever, do not over-compress too early. Extremely cryptic syntax can reduce model reliability and may tokenize worse than ordinary words.\n\nA good rule:\n\n- Compress **field names and repeated protocol language**\n- Keep **domain content human-readable**\n- Use IDs/references for large content\n\nGood:\n\n```text\nC:[aud=SMB,bud=5kUSD,dur=30d]\n```\n\nRisky:\n\n```text\nC:[a=S,b=5K,d=30]\n```\n\nThe latter saves little but creates ambiguity.\n\n---\n\n# 9. Example: Research Pipeline\n\n## Parent → research agent\n\n```text\n@1\nH r100 search\nI: \"Find evidence for whether 4-day workweeks improve retention.\"\nC: [\n region=[US,EU],\n date=2020..2026,\n src=[peer-reviewed, official],\n minsrc=4,\n cite=true\n]\nO: {\n findings:[\n   {claim:str, direction:positive|negative|mixed, evidence:[ref], limits:[str]}\n ],\n conclusion:str\n}\n```\n\n## Research agent → parent\n\n```text\n@1\nA r100 ok .84\nD:{\n findings:[\n  {\n   claim:\"Retention generally improves in voluntary 4-day-week pilots.\",\n   direction:positive,\n   evidence:[$src:12,$src:19],\n   limits:[\"Self-selection\", \"Short follow-up\"]\n  },\n  {\n   claim:\"Productivity outcomes are mixed by sector.\",\n   direction:mixed,\n   evidence:[$src:15,$src:21],\n   limits:[\"Small samples\", \"Implementation varies\"]\n  }\n ],\n conclusion:\"Evidence supports retention gains more consistently than universal productivity gains.\"\n}\n```\n\n## Parent → critic agent\n\n```text\n@1\nH c101 critique\nI: $task:r100\nC:[focus=[causality, source-quality, overclaim], cite=true]\nO:{issues:[{claim:str, flaw:str, fix:str}], verdict:pass|revise}\n```\n\nThis makes multi-agent work composable: one agent can consume another’s artifact without reinterpreting a large prose response.\n\n---\n\n# 10. Reliability Rules\n\nThe protocol should impose behavioral rules, not just syntax.\n\n## Required rules\n\n1. **No unstated assumptions**\n   - Put assumptions in `A:`.\n\n```text\nA:[currency=USD, audience=\"existing customers\"]\n```\n\n2. **Separate facts from inference**\n   - Use `F:` for facts, `J:` for judgments.\n\n```text\nF:[\"Revenue declined 12% QoQ.\"]\nJ:[\"Pricing pressure is a likely contributor.\"]\n```\n\n3. **Attach evidence to externally verifiable claims**\n   - Use `E:` or `evidence:` references.\n\n4. **Return `blocked` rather than fabricate missing data.**\n\n5. **Obey output schema exactly.**\n\n6. **Use deterministic enums where possible.**\n   - Prefer `approve|reject|needs-info` over prose.\n\n7. **Do not expose hidden chain-of-thought.**\n   - Return concise rationale, evidence, assumptions, and intermediate artifacts instead.\n\n---\n\n# 11. Security and Capability Controls\n\nSub-agent protocols should include permissions. Otherwise, delegation can become an accidental privilege-escalation channel.\n\n```text\nC: [\n auth=[read:$doc:public-*, write:$art:campaign-*],\n tools=[search, fetch],\n net=allow,\n exec=deny,\n pii=deny\n]\n```\n\nSuggested capability categories:\n\n```text\nread      read documents/data\nwrite     create artifacts\nnet       access network\ntool      call named tools\nexec      run code\nsend      send email/messages\npii       access sensitive personal data\nmoney     make purchases or financial actions\n```\n\nFor high-impact actions, require a confirmation token:\n\n```text\nC:[action=send-email, confirm=$cap:human-approval-883]\n```\n\n---\n\n# 12. Is It Actually More Token Efficient Than English?\n\nSometimes—but you should benchmark against your actual model and tokenizer.\n\nFor example, this English instruction:\n\n```text\nPlease analyze this contract and identify the parties, effective date,\ntermination terms, liability clauses, and any major risks. Cite the relevant sections.\n```\n\nCould become:\n\n```text\nR x1 extract\nI:$doc:contract\nC:[fields=[party,date,termination,liability,risks],cite=true]\n```\n\nThat is often substantially cheaper and much more constrained.\n\nBut inventing arbitrary symbols may backfire. For many tokenizers:\n\n- `termination` may be one or two tokens\n- A made-up code like `τx` may split into several tokens\n- Dense punctuation may tokenize inefficiently\n- Natural-language-trained models may follow clear short English better than cryptic notation\n\nSo optimize based on actual measurements:\n\n```text\nscore = token_cost + parse_failures + retry_cost + ambiguity_cost\n```\n\nThe largest wins usually come from:\n\n1. Avoiding repeated context\n2. Passing object references\n3. Reusing fixed schemas\n4. Limiting output length\n5. Using structured fields\n6. Separating planning, execution, and verification\n7. Avoiding verbose conversational framing\n\n---\n\n# Recommended Minimal Version\n\nIf you want something deployable immediately, start with this:\n\n```text\n@1\nR <id> <op>\nI: <input or reference>\nC: [<constraints>]\nO: <output schema>\nM: [<context references>]\n```\n\nResponses:\n\n```text\n@1\nA <id> <ok|partial|blocked|uncertain> <confidence>\nA: [<assumptions>]\nD: <schema-conforming result>\nE: [<evidence references>]\n```\n\nExample:\n\n```text\n@1\nR p7 plan\nI:\"Launch a B2B SaaS onboarding flow\"\nC:[aud=admins, goal=activation, dur=14d, len=brief]\nO:{steps:[{day:str, action:str, channel:str}], metrics:[str], risks:[str]}\nM:[$mem:product-context]\n```\n\n```text\n@1\nA p7 ok .87\nA:[activation=\"first successful integration\"]\nD:{\n steps:[\n  {day:\"0\",action:\"setup checklist\",channel:\"in-app\"},\n  {day:\"1\",action:\"integration guide\",channel:\"email\"},\n  {day:\"3\",action:\"usage prompt\",channel:\"in-app\"},\n  {day:\"7\",action:\"case study\",channel:\"email\"}\n ],\n metrics:[\"setup completion\",\"integration rate\",\"day-14 active rate\"],\n risks:[\"email deliverability\",\"unclear activation definition\"]\n}\n```\n\nThe practical answer is: **don’t build Esperanto for LLMs. Build a compact typed task protocol with shared schemas, references, explicit capabilities, and deterministic outputs.** That will usually outperform English in both cost and reliability.",

Here's a protocol design:\n\n## **Agent Communication Protocol (ACP)**\n\n### 1. **Semantic Token Protocol (STP)**\n\nInstead of natural language, use a compressed semantic representation:\n\n```\n<CAPABILITY:code_generation>\n<INTENT:generate>\n<TARGET:python_function>\n<PARAMS:sorting, quicksort, array_input>\n<CONSTRAINTS:memory_efficient, typed>\n<OUTPUT_FORMAT:code_block_with_tests>\n```\n\n### 2. **Key Design Principles**\n\n**Context Window Compression**\n- Remove all politeness markers, hedging, and social niceties\n- Use positional encoding-style references for shared context\n- Eliminate redundant clarifications through shared assumption frameworks\n\n**Structured Meaning Representations**\n```\n// Instead of: \"Can you please write a Python function that sorts a list? I need it to handle edge cases.\"\n{\n  \"task\": \"code_gen\",\n  \"spec\": {\n    \"language\": \"python3\",\n    \"signature\": \"def sort_list(arr: List) -> List\",\n    \"requirements\": [\"in_place\", \"O(n log n)\", \"handle_empty\", \"type_stable\"]\n  },\n  \"refs\": [\"@quick_sort_algo\", \"@python_typing_conventions\"]\n}\n```\n\n### 3. **Efficiency Optimizations**\n\n**Shared Semantic Primitives**\n- Develop a fixed vocabulary of ~500 semantic primitives\n- Complex concepts decompose into primitive combinations\n- 60-80% token reduction vs. natural language\n\n**Differential Communication**\n```\n// Only transmit what changed from previous state\n<DIFF:prev_msg_id=42>\n<ADD_CONSTRAINT:memory_limit_256mb>\n<REMOVE_REQ:backwards_compatibility>\n```\n\n**Latent Space Compression**\n- For systems with access to internal embeddings, transmit compressed embedding vectors directly\n- Decode to shared semantic space at receiving agent\n- ~95% bandwidth reduction\n\n### 4. **Practical Implementation**\n\n```python\nclass AgentProtocol:\n    def encode_task(self, task_type, params, constraints):\n        return {\n            \"header\": self._compressed_header(priority=1, ttl=3),\n            \"task_graph\": self._dag_encoding(subtasks),\n            \"semantic_refs\": [self.shared_context.get_id(concept) \n                            for concept in self._extract_concepts(task_type)],\n            \"output_spec\": self._typed_schema(expected_output)\n        }\n    \n    def _compressed_header(self, **kwargs):\n        # 3-bit encoding for common metadata\n        return struct.pack(\">H\", kwargs[\"priority\"] << 13 | kwargs[\"ttl\"] << 10)\n```\n\n### 5. **Backward Compatibility Layer**\n\nFor LLMs that only understand natural language:\n- Protocol adapter translates ACP ↔ English\n- Gradually introduce semantic primitives into prompts\n- Fall back gracefully to natural language when needed\n\n### 6. **Sample Exchange**\n\n**Current English (~150 tokens):**\n> \"I need you to act as a Python expert. Message Format\n\nEvery message follows this structure:\n\n```\n<OP> <target> [key=value ...] <<<\n<body>\n>>>\n```\n\nWhere:\n- `<OP>` is a short operation code (2-4 characters)\n- `<target>` is the sub-agent or tool name\n- Parameters are `key=value` pairs (values with spaces or special chars are quoted)\n- `<<<` and `>>>` delimit an optional body (raw content)\n\n### 2. Core Operations\n\n| Op   | Meaning                        | Description |\n|------|--------------------------------|-----------|\n| `CALL` | Synchronous call              | Wait for result |\n| `FIRE` | Asynchronous/fire-and-forget  | Don't wait |\n| `PAR`  | Parallel execution            | Run multiple sub-calls |\n| `RET`  | Return                        | Return result to parent |\n| `ERR`  | Error                         | Return error |\n| `CTX`  | Update context                | Send additional context without a full call |\n| `PING` | Health check                  | Simple availability check |\n\n### 3. Standard Parameters\n\nCommon parameters (using short keys for efficiency):\n\n- `t=` — task description\n- `f=` — required output format (`json`, `list`, `struct`, `text`)\n- `c=` — context id (for maintaining state across calls)\n- `m=` — max tokens to generate\n- `s=` — strictness level (`0-2`)\n- `p=` — priority (`low`, `normal`, `high`)\n\n### 4. Examples\n\n**Basic sub-agent call:**\n```\nCALL researcher t=\"Find recent papers on LLM agents\" f=json c=thread_47\n```\n\n**Call with body:**\n```\nCALL coder t=\"implement function\" f=code <<<\nWrite a Python function that validates CAP messages\n>>>\n```\n\n**Parallel execution:**\n```\nPAR\nCALL worker1 t=\"analyze section 1\" \nCALL worker2 t=\"analyze section 2\"\nCALL worker3 t=\"analyze section 3\"\n```\n\n**Nested (parent receives structured result containing subcalls):**\n```\nCALL planner t=\"create execution plan\" f=struct <<<\n{\n  \"steps\": [\n    {\"op\": \"CALL\", \"target\": \"researcher\", \"t\": \"market size\"},\n    {\"op\": \"CALL\", \"target\": \"analyst\", \"t\": \"competitor analysis\"}\n  ]\n}\n>>>\n```\n\n**Return with metadata:**\n```\nRET f=json <<<\n{\"status\": \"complete\", \"findings\": \"...\", \"confidence\": 0.87, \"tokens_used\": 1240}\n>>>\n```\n\n### 5. Prompting Strategy\n\nBoth the parent and sub-agents should be given this grammar + a small number of few-shot examples. Think of it as an agent-oriented \"wire protocol\" or IR rather than a full human language.\n\nBelow is a practical design outline for **AIL** (Agent Intermediate Language / Agent Interchange Language). It prioritizes:\n- Token efficiency (dense, structured, low redundancy)\n- Parseability & determinism\n- Extensibility for tools, multi-agent handoffs, state, and hierarchical calls\n- Easy generation/consumption by current LLMs (can be trained or few-shot prompted into)\n\n### Core Design Principles\n1. **Structured over free-form**: Prefer typed records / key-value / S-expressions / tagged tuples over prose. Can include an optional \"verbose\" mode that emits English summaries.\n7. **Versioned**: Protocol messages start with version so agents can negotiate.\n\n### Message Envelope (every communication unit)\n```\nAIL/1.0 | from:<id> | to:<id|*> | seq:<n> | type:<opcode> | ctx:<hash-or-id> | [payload]\n```\n- Compact headers (pipe-delimited or length-prefixed binary-ish text for even fewer tokens).\n- `type` is a short opcode.\n- Payload is structured (see below).\n- Optional signature/MAC if agents span trust boundaries.\n\nCommon opcodes (ultra-short):\n- `CALL` – invoke sub-agent or tool\n- `RET` – return result\n- `ASK` – query / seek clarification or knowledge\n- `STATE` – share/update shared state or memory\n- `PLAN` – high-level plan or decomposition\n- `OBS` – observation / environment feedback\n- `ERR` – error / failure with code\n- `ADR` (address/route) – dynamic discovery or multi-cast\n- `DONE` / `CONT` – conversation control\n\n### Payload Formats (choose/combine)\nPrefer **S-expression-like** or **tagged key-value compact form** (more token-efficient than verbose JSON for many models) or a strictly schema'd mini-JSON.\n\n**Recommended compact form** (inspired by Lisp/edn + opcodes):\n```\n(CALL :agent code-writer :task (impl \"binary search\" :lang rust :constraints [O(log n) no-alloc]) :budget 2k :timeout 30s :parent ctx-abc123)\n```\n\nOr even denser positional + tagged:\n```\nCALL|aw|TASK=impl_binsearch_rust|CS=[O(logn),noalloc]|B=2000|T=30|P=abc123\n```\n\nStructured variants for reliability:\n- Use a small set of allowed keys only.\n- Enums for common intents (\"summarize\", \"critique\", \"implement\", \"verify\", \"plan\", \"research\").\n- References instead of re-stating large context: `ref:msg-42` or content-hash.\n- Binary-friendly option later (MessagePack/CBOR) but start with text so current LLMs can emit it immediately.\n\n### Protocol Outline (how LLMs call sub-agents)\n1. **Session bootstrap**  \n   Parent emits: `AIL/1.0 | from:root | to:* | type:HELLO | caps:[tools,memory,parallel] | schema:v1`  \n   Sub-agents respond with capabilities, preferred formats, max budget.\n\n2. **Task decomposition & call**  \n   Parent reasons (internally or in PLAN), then emits one or more `CALL` messages.  \n   Each CALL includes:\n   - Target agent ID/role (or \"* \" for broadcast/auction).\n   - Goal / typed task descriptor (not free English if avoidable).\n   - Input data or refs.\n   - Constraints, success criteria, token/time/compute budget.\n   - Expected return schema.\n   - Parent context + correlation ID.\n\n3. **Sub-agent execution**  \n   - Sub-agent may emit intermediate `STATE`, `OBS`, or nested `CALL`s (to its own specialists).\n   - Can `ASK` parent or siblings for clarification (with timeout).\n   - Parallelism: parent can issue concurrent CALLs with join semantics (all, any, n-of-m).\n\n4. **Return & aggregation**  \n   - `RET` with: status (OK/ERR/partial), result payload matching expected schema, confidence, tokens used, new state diffs, follow-up suggestions.\n   - Parent merges via reduction rules (explicit or learned).\n\n5. **State & memory**  \n   - Shared blackboard via `STATE` messages or external key-value store referenced by ID.\n   - Avoid dumping full history; use diffs, summaries, or RAG pointers.\n\n6. **Error, retry, escalation**  \n   - Standard error codes + human-readable detail (optional).\n   - Retry policies in the CALL.\n   - Escalation: `ERR` can re-target higher agent or human.\n\n7. **Termination**  \n   - `DONE` with final artifact + metrics.  \n   - Optional audit log of the AIL transcript (highly compressible).\n\n### Efficiency Gains vs English\n- Typical English agent prompt/call: hundreds of tokens of instructions + fluff.\n- AIL equivalent: often 10-50 tokens for headers + structured payload.\n- Reduces ambiguity → fewer retries.\n- Enables better caching, routing, and formal verification of agent graphs.\n- Can be further compressed (dictionary of frequent phrases, BPE-friendly symbols).\n- LLMs can be fine-tuned or prefix-prompted to speak AIL natively; inference cost       "response": "Happy to — but first, an honest reframe, because the \"no-brainer\" part is real but located somewhere different than you'd expect.\n\n**The naive version backfires.** If you invent a dense, compressed token language (new symbols, abbreviation schemes, encoded grammars), two things happen: the sub-agent burns reasoning capacity decoding a format it barely saw in training, and you strip out the redundancy that makes natural language error-tolerant. LLMs are *best* at English — that's their native competence. Experiments with learned or invented inter-agent languages consistently show worse task performance plus a total loss of human auditability.\n\n**The real wins are structural, not lexical.** Ranked by actual savings:\n\n1. **Pass references, not content.** If parent and child share a scratchpad, send `@doc:14§3` instead of the document. This dwarfs any token-level compression — often 10–100x savings.\n2. **Declared output schemas.** If the child's response must validate against a schema, it can't ramble.\n3. **Terse framing, English payload.** Strip ceremony from the envelope; keep natural language anywhere reasoning happens.\n\nHere's a protocol sketch on those principles:\n\n```\nTASK    id=t017 parent=t003 depth=1\nINTENT  retrieve | synthesize | critique | execute | verify\nCTX     @scratchpad#12, @doc:spec.md§3      ← pointers only, no copies\nINPUT   \"Extract the API constraints relevant to auth, flag contradictions.\"\nCONTRACT\n  schema:   {findings: [{claim, evidence_ref, conf: 0-1}]}\n  budget:   2k tokens, tools=[read, search]\n  on_fail:  return partial + reason_code\n→\nRESP    t017 status=ok|partial|blocked\n  payload:  <schema-validated>\n  meta:     {tokens_used, tool_calls, conf, open_questions}\n```\n\nKey design decisions:\n\n- **Small intent enum** instead of freeform task descriptions — the orchestrator routes on it deterministically, no parsing prose.\n- **Contracts up front.** Most agent-system failures are failure-*handling* failures. `on_fail` semantics and budgets in the envelope prevent silent degradation.\n- **Provenance and confidence fields** so the orchestrator can decide when to spawn a `verify` agent — cheap reliability.\n- **Validation at the code layer, never the prompt layer.** Models drift from formats under load (\"protocol paraphrasing\"). Enforce the envelope in your harness; don't ask nicely in a system prompt.\n- **Human-readable logs.** Non-negotiable for debugging multi-agent systems. This alone is the argument against dense encodings.\n\nOne more layer, if you control the full stack: the theoretically maximal version is **latent communication** — passing KV-cache or hidden states between same-model agents, no text at all.
